one_line_takeaway: MEA-Defender embeds a 'symbiotic backdoor' watermark whose input and output feature domains both live inside the main-task distribution, so it is stolen along with the main task during model extraction, yielding 83%+ WSR on stolen models (vs 3.37% for Entangled Watermark) and resisting fine-tuning, pruning, Neural Cleanse and ABS, though only under soft-label attacks.
key_finding: The symbiotic backdoor transfers into stolen models where prior watermarks do not. Average WSR on extracted models is 83.25% (SL avg 82.84%, SSL avg 84.36%), far above the 30% detection threshold, and 64.04% (paper also reports 66.72% avg in the architecture-mismatch table) even when the extracted model uses a DIFFERENT architecture than the victim. Against Entangled Watermark on the different-architecture extracted model, MEA-Defender reaches 83.54% WSR versus Entangled Watermark's 3.37%. The ablation shows both loss components are necessary: Lcom-only yields ~1.08% extracted WSR, Lver-only 26.95% (below threshold), and both together 76.20%. The evasion loss cuts the false-positive rate on decoy samples from ~48-52% down to ~5%. The watermark also resists post-extraction removal/detection: 81.05% WSR after fine-tuning, 69.68% after pruning, 0% detection by Neural Cleanse, and only 9.9% average trigger success by ABS reverse-engineering.
core_contribution: MEA-Defender is a black-box DNN watermark designed to survive model extraction attacks, protecting the intellectual property of both supervised-learning (SL) models and self-supervised-learning (SSL) encoders. Its core novelty is a 'symbiotic backdoor': a watermark sample is a single image synthesized by combining the input features of two samples from two different source classes (e.g., Automobile + Airplane) and assigned a distinct target label (e.g., Bird). This makes the watermark's INPUT domain an indispensable subset of the main-task input distribution. A dedicated watermark loss then forces the watermark samples' OUTPUT feature vectors to lie within the distribution of the main-task samples' output features (specifically between/among the two source classes' feature clusters). Because both the input and output domains of the watermark are 'symbiotic' with the main task, an adversary who extracts the main task via querying necessarily extracts the watermark along with it. The paper claims to be the first watermark that (a) works for both SL and SSL, (b) does NOT assume the extracted model shares the victim's architecture, and (c) preserves normal use by benign users. This is distinct from the cited prior defenses it compares against (Entangled Watermark, SSLGuard, DAWN, Composite Backdoor) which are Related Work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: The adversary accesses the victim model only in a black-box manner and performs a model extraction (stealing) attack: querying the victim with in- or out-of-distribution samples, collecting the returned SOFT-LABEL confidence vectors, and training a substitute (extracted) model that mimics the main task. The adversary does NOT know the victim's architecture, so the extracted model may use a different architecture. The adversary's goal is to shed any embedded watermark to reuse/commercialize the stolen model without IP-infringement liability. The paper additionally evaluates 'synthesized attacks' (extract first, then apply watermark removal/detection: fine-tuning, pruning, mislabelling mixed samples, Neural Cleanse, ABS) and a stronger white-box removal threat where the adversary has full access to the victim's parameters/architecture and tries fine-tuning, transfer learning, pruning, Neural Cleanse, ABS, anomaly detection, and input preprocessing. The defender (owner) verifies ownership by black-box querying a suspect model with secret watermark samples and computing Watermark Success Rate (WSR); WSR above a 30% threshold signals IP infringement. Owner secrets are the two source labels, the combination configuration, and the target label.
limitations: 1) The watermark only survives in the SOFT-LABEL extraction setting (adversary obtains confidence vectors); the authors explicitly state it CANNOT survive hard-label extraction, because the method manipulates the decision boundary and confidence vectors carry more boundary information than bare labels. 2) Security rests partly on the secrecy of the combining approach, its configuration, and the two source labels; if an adversary learns these, stealthiness arguments weaken (the paper argues brute-forcing is expensive but does not prove hardness). 3) WSR on extracted models, while above threshold, is far from the 100% on victim models and drops further under architecture mismatch (as low as ~54.95% in some cells of Table 2), so the safety margin over the 30% threshold is modest in adverse cases. 4) Autoencoder-based blending is the weakest combiner (60.74% extracted WSR) due to injected noise. 5) Reliance on a fixed empirically-set 30% threshold; robustness of that threshold across tasks/architectures beyond those tested is asserted rather than broadly guaranteed. 6) Evaluation is confined to vision/NLP/speech classification-style tasks and moderate-scale models; no evaluation on large modern generative models.

quantitative_results: Average WSR on extracted models: 83.25% (SL 82.84%, SSL 84.36%) vs 30% IP-infringement threshold
WSR with different extracted-vs-victim architecture: 64.04% (Table 2 avg reported as 66.72%), min cell 54.95%
MEA-Defender vs Entangled Watermark on different-architecture extracted model: 83.54% vs 3.37% WSR
MEA-Defender vs Entangled Watermark on same-architecture extracted model: 71.05% vs 18.74% WSR
MEA-Defender vs Composite Backdoor on extracted models: 76.20% vs 26.95% (same arch), 69.18% vs 28.92% (different arch)
Victim-model WSR is 100.00% across all tasks; main-task accuracy examples: CIFAR-10 81.82%, Youtube Face 99.70%, AG News 88.15%
Ablation of watermark loss (extracted WSR): Lcom-only 1.08%, Lver-only 26.95%, Lcom+Lver 76.20%
Evasion loss effect on WFPR (decoy mixed samples): 5.32% victim / 5.26% extracted WITH evasion loss vs 51.68% / 48.10% WITHOUT
Post-extraction removal robustness: 81.05% WSR after fine-tuning, 69.68% after pruning (80% prune rate figures 63-87% by task)
Detection resistance: Neural Cleanse 0% detection of watermark label; ABS reversed-trigger success only 9.9% avg (15.15% victim / 10.12% extracted for Bird label)

White-box removal: WSR still >70% after 100-epoch fine-tuning; 67.50% after transfer to STL-10; >63.90% at 80% pruning; anomaly detection catches only 2.94% of watermark samples; input preprocessing leaves 97.00% (SL) / 96.80% (SSL) WSR
Query-set effect (Knockoff): in-distribution CIFAR-10 gives 97.93% WSR at 81.70% acc; out-of-distribution avg 86.43% WSR (Caltech-256 acc collapses to 22.51%, WSR 65.44%)
Piracy attack: owner watermark WSR 74.4% vs pirate watermark 7.4% on the extracted model
Combining approaches (extracted WSR): cropping/pasting 76.20%, autoencoder blending 60.74%, pixel merging 77.72%, stripe combination 72.00%
SSLGuard comparison: MEA-Defender encoder 81.50% CIFAR-10 acc vs SSLGuard 76.50%; extracted-encoder WSR 79.84% (same arch) / 84.16% (different arch)
baselines_compared: Entangled Watermark (Jia et al., USENIX Sec 2021) - SL only
SSLGuard (Cong et al.) - SSL encoders only
Composite Backdoor (Lin et al., CCS 2020) - repurposed as SL watermark
Li et al. (AAAI 2022, verifying embedded external features) - discussed/tabulated
Extraction attacks used as attackers: Hinton knowledge distillation, Papernot et al., Knockoff Nets, StolenEncoder
Removal/detection attacks: Fine-tuning, Pruning, Neural Cleanse, ABS, transfer learning, anomaly detection (LOF), input preprocessing
